policy_statement: Develop a fossil-fuel plants transition strategy by 2024.
mechanism_description: Developing a transition strategy for fossil-fuel power plants enables their phaseout and replacement with cleaner generation, reducing electricity-sector greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Electricity generation planning/asset retirement → ↓ fossil generation (coal/oil/gas) + ↑ low/zero-carbon supply → ↓ CO₂e emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Utilities/cities develop and implement transition plans for fossil-fuel power plants (retirement schedules, replacement procurement, workforce/community transition), shifting the generation mix away from fossil units.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced combustion of fossil fuels in power plants lowers direct stack CO₂ (and some CH₄/N₂O) emissions; replacement with renewables/nuclear/clean firm power avoids those combustion emissions per kWh.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in grid emissions intensity and total electricity-sector CO₂e emissions (mitigation).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced electricity-sector GHG emissions from retiring/repowering fossil generation and replacing it with cleaner sources.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: planning enables fossil plant phaseout and clean replacement, which primarily reduces GHG emissions from electricity generation.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: the main intended and largest climate effect is emissions reduction, not primarily improving efficiency (resource efficiency), managing hazards (adaptation), or enhancing ecosystems (nature-based solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: while transition plans can include reliability/resilience components or workforce/equity measures, these are co-benefits; the core climate mechanism remains grid decarbonization via fossil displacement.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: any “fossil power plant transition planning → grid decarbonization” policy is fundamentally aimed at reducing emissions by enabling retirement/repowering and clean replacement, so Mitigation is consistently primary.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: any further subtype (e.g., coal vs gas, renewable vs clean firm replacement, just-transition emphasis) must be assigned based on policy-specific evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some transition strategies may emphasize local air quality, affordability, or grid resilience; if a plan lacks credible retirement/replacement actions it could weaken realized mitigation, but the mechanism as defined is still primarily mitigation via emissions reduction.
co_benefits: Potential air quality and public health improvements from reduced combustion (SO₂/NOₓ/PM); potential grid reliability and cost planning benefits; potential just-transition/workforce and community economic support (not specified in text).
instance_edge_case_notes: No explicit retirement/phaseout mandate, emissions target, or clean procurement requirement is stated—only development of a strategy—so it is classified as planning-strategy rather than regulatory-mandate/target-setting.
